aliases: Steamed Tapioca Dumpling with Pork and Shrimp

english: Steamed Tapioca Dumpling with Pork and Shrimp

region_of_origin: Vietnam (Central Vietnam, Huế origin; served in Singapore)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Vietnamese tapioca dumpling (Bánh Bột Lọc). The closest defensible FoodOn anchor is the generic 'dumpling food product' class (FOODON_03315671). A more specific term for tapioca-wrapped steamed dumplings with pork and shrimp filling is absent from the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_description: Composite dishes — dumplings and similar filled pasta/dough products
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Vietnamese tapioca dumplings. A0FJP (composite dishes — dumplings) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 ancestor. Tapioca-specific wrapper and Vietnamese origin are not captured at this level of the hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Vietnamese tapioca dumpling or Bánh Bột Lọc as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Bánh Bột Lọc consists of 3 to 4 individual dumplings weighing approximately 100 g, served with sweet fish sauce (nước chấm) on the side.

nutrient_source.title: Vietnamese Tapioca Dumpling (Bánh Bột Lọc) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Vietnamese Tapioca Dumpling (Bánh Bột Lọc), cr_id G00010624, with confidence 0.95. All core macros and available micronutrients are taken from this record. Saturated fat and dietary fibre are estimated from ingredient composition (tapioca starch, pork belly, shrimp) using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as a secondary reference for fatty acid breakdown, as HPB does not report these sub-fractions for this entry.

gi_source.title: Tapioca (boiled) — Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load values
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Bánh Bột Lọc in the University of Sydney GI Database. The GI of 70 is estimated using boiled tapioca as the closest analogous food (GI ~70, University of Sydney GI Database). The tapioca starch wrapper is the dominant carbohydrate source in this dish. The pork and shrimp filling contribute negligible carbohydrate. Glycemic load per 100 g serving = (70 × 8.03) / 100 = 5.6, classified as low GL despite high GI due to the low total carbohydrate content per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (46 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: The dumpling itself is very low in sodium at 46 mg/100 g; however, the accompanying nước chấm dipping sauce typically contains fish sauce and added sugar, which can substantially increase total sodium intake — patients with hypertension should request a reduced-sodium sauce or use it sparingly.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI tapioca starch wrapper with moderate carbohydrate (8 g/100 g) and high protein (8.71 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Tapioca starch has a high glycemic index (~70), but the low total carbohydrate per serving (8 g/100 g) results in a low glycemic load of 5.6; the high protein content (8.71 g/100 g) further blunts post-meal glucose rise — patients with Type 2 Diabetes should control portion size, avoid sweet dipping sauces, and monitor post-meal glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~1.2 g/100 g), low sodium (46 mg/100 g), and moderate cholesterol (28 mg/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Bánh Bột Lọc has a modest saturated fat content (~1.2 g/100 g) and low sodium (46 mg/100 g), making it a relatively heart-friendly choice in moderate portions; patients with cardiovascular disease should limit the sweet fish sauce accompaniment to control sodium and added sugar intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork belly filling contains arachidonic acid (omega-6 fatty acid) which can amplify inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The pork belly component of this dumpling is a source of arachidonic acid, which may exacerbate joint inflammation in rheumatoid arthritis patients — consider requesting a shrimp-only filling and pairing with anti-inflammatory herbs such as ginger or turmeric in the dipping sauce.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bánh Bột Lọc (Vietnamese Tapioca Dumpling) is a traditional Vietnamese steamed dumpling originating from Huế in Central Vietnam, widely enjoyed in Vietnam and available in Vietnamese restaurants in Singapore. The dumpling wrapper is made from tapioca starch (bột lọc), which becomes translucent and slightly chewy when steamed, giving the dumpling a distinctive crystal-clear appearance. The filling typically consists of seasoned pork belly and whole shrimp, flavoured with fish sauce, shallots, and black pepper. The dumplings are served with nước chấm, a sweet-savoury Vietnamese dipping sauce made from fish sauce, lime juice, sugar, garlic, and chilli. Per 100 g, Bánh Bột Lọc provides 101 kcal, 8.71 g protein, 3.78 g fat, and 8.03 g carbohydrate, with very low sodium (46 mg) and low sugar (0.21 g). The tapioca starch wrapper has a high glycemic index (~70) but the low total carbohydrate content per serving results in a low glycemic load (~5.6), making portion control the key dietary consideration. The dish is gluten-free and dairy-free. Clinically, it is suitable for hypertension management due to its very low sodium content, though the dipping sauce must be used sparingly. For Type 2 Diabetes, the high protein content mitigates the glycaemic impact of the tapioca starch. The pork belly filling is a source of arachidonic acid and may be of concern for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis or inflammatory conditions. Known in Chinese as 越南木薯粉饺, in Malay as Dumpling Tapioka Vietnam, and widely recognised by its Vietnamese name Bánh Bột Lọc across Singapore's multicultural food scene.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Vietnamese tapioca dumpling?
Is Bánh Bột Lọc suitable for diabetics?
What is the GI of tapioca dumpling?
Is Bánh Bột Lọc high in sodium?
What is in the filling of Bánh Bột Lọc?
Can hypertension patients eat Vietnamese tapioca dumpling?
What is the difference between Bánh Bột Lọc and other Vietnamese dumplings?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010624) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The source is an approved primary regional source for Singapore food records. Saturated fat and dietary fibre are estimated from ingredient composition using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as a secondary reference. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (boiled tapioca) in the University of Sydney GI Database.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.2 g/100 g) is estimated from ingredient composition (pork belly and shrimp proportions typical of Bánh Bột Lọc) using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy fatty acid data, as HPB does not report saturated fat sub-fractions for this entry. 2. Dietary fibre (0.3 g/100 g) is estimated from ingredient composition; tapioca starch contributes negligible fibre and the small amounts of shallot and shrimp contribute trace fibre. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid sub-fractions, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this traditional Vietnamese preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 4. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate based on boiled tapioca (University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement for Bánh Bột Lọc exists in the approved GI source database. Glycemic load per serving = (70 × 8.03) / 100 = 5.6 (low GL). 5. Halal status is not confirmed as pork belly is a primary filling ingredient — this dish is not suitable for halal dietary requirements. 6. The food_group 'Fish and seafood' and food_subgroup 'Fish-based dishes' are preserved from the HPB source record; the dish contains both pork and shrimp, so 'Mixed dishes' could also be argued, but the HPB classification is retained for canonical consistency. 7. Chinese names (越南木薯粉饺 simplified; 越南木薯粉餃 traditional), Malay name (Dumpling Tapioka Vietnam), Tamil name (வியட்நாமிய தபியோகா டம்ப்ளிங்), and Hindi name (वियतनामी टैपिओका डम्पलिंग) added to satisfy Singapore HPB record multilingual naming requirements. These are descriptive translations of the English name into the respective languages. 8. Ontology match types corrected to 'closest_parent' for both FoodOn and FoodEx2, as no exact match exists for this specific dish in either ontology.
